content.text: Why Blueprints Must Own Interface Standards

An enterprise GenAI system integrates with API gateways, upstream event producers, downstream consumers, audit log pipelines, and monitoring collectors — often simultaneously. Each integration creates a contract: a shared agreement between the GenAI system and its counterpart about what data looks like, how authentication works, how errors are communicated, and how schema evolution is managed.

When interface contracts are left to individual delivery teams to define ad hoc, the enterprise accumulates heterogeneous APIs that are difficult to govern, secure, and evolve. A blueprint that specifies interface standards eliminates this variation: every system built from the blueprint exposes the same shapes, speaks the same authentication protocols, and uses the same error vocabulary.

Interface contracts are the most expensive thing to change after go-live. Consumer-driven contract tests catch breaking changes in CI, not in production — but only if the contract was defined up front.

attrs.alt: GenAI integration interface standards — layered view showing consumers, the six standard layers (transport, auth, schema, async, versioning, contract testing), and the GenAI system

content.text: Synchronous Interface Standards
Transport and Protocol
The blueprint specifies which transport protocols are permitted for synchronous consumer-facing interfaces:
Protocol
Use Case
Standard Constraint
REST/JSON over HTTPS
Primary consumer API for web, mobile, and service-to-service calls
TLS 1.3 minimum; HTTP/2 required for server-sent events; HTTP/1.1 permitted for simple request-response only
Server-Sent Events (SSE)
Streaming token-by-token LLM output to browser clients
Connection timeout 60s; reconnect via Last-Event-ID header; must respect auth on reconnect
gRPC over HTTP/2
High-throughput service-to-service calls (embedding, inference)
Protobuf schema versioned in a shared schema registry; mutual TLS required for internal calls
WebSocket
Conversational UI with bidirectional streaming
Auth token re-validated on connection upgrade; connection idle timeout 5 minutes
Standard Request and Response Schema

All synchronous APIs exposed by systems built from this blueprint share a common request envelope and response envelope. The envelope carries fields that support distributed tracing, audit, and consistent client error handling:

# OpenAPI 3.1 — shared request/response schema components
# Stored in: schema-registry/genai-common/v1.yaml

components:
  schemas:
    # ── Request headers (required on all calls) ──────────────────────
    StandardRequestHeaders:
      type: object
      required: [X-Correlation-ID, X-Request-ID]
      properties:
        X-Correlation-ID:
          type: string
          format: uuid
          description: Trace ID propagated from the originating request (for distributed tracing)
        X-Request-ID:
          type: string
          format: uuid
          description: Unique ID for this specific request (idempotency and dedup)
        X-Client-Version:
          type: string
          description: Caller application version (for telemetry)

    # ── Synchronous query request ─────────────────────────────────────
    QueryRequest:
      type: object
      required: [query, session_id]
      properties:
        query:
          type: string
          maxLength: 4000
          description: User query text (after client-side sanitisation)
        session_id:
          type: string
          format: uuid
          description: Conversation session (for multi-turn context)
        max_tokens:
          type: integer
          minimum: 1
          maximum: 2000
          default: 800
        stream:
          type: boolean
          default: false
          description: If true, response uses SSE; if false, waits for full answer

    # ── Standard success response ─────────────────────────────────────
    QueryResponse:
      type: object
      required: [answer, citations, request_id, latency_ms]
      properties:
        answer:
          type: string
        citations:
          type: array
          items:
            $ref: '#/components/schemas/Citation'
        confidence:
          type: number
          minimum: 0
          maximum: 1
          description: Composite confidence score from routing layer
        routed_to_human_review:
          type: boolean
          description: True if output is pending human review (async delivery)
        request_id:
          type: string
          format: uuid
        latency_ms:
          type: integer

    Citation:
      type: object
      required: [doc_id, title, relevance_score]
      properties:
        doc_id:   { type: string }
        title:    { type: string }
        section:  { type: string }
        relevance_score: { type: number }

    # ── Standard error envelope ───────────────────────────────────────
    ErrorResponse:
      type: object
      required: [error_code, message, request_id, timestamp]
      properties:
        error_code:
          type: string
          enum:
            - RATE_LIMITED
            - INVALID_INPUT
            - AUTH_FAILED
            - CONTEXT_OVERFLOW
            - LLM_UNAVAILABLE
            - CONTENT_POLICY_VIOLATION
            - HUMAN_REVIEW_REQUIRED   # output held; poll status endpoint
            - INTERNAL_ERROR
        message:
          type: string
          description: Human-readable, safe to surface to the end user
        detail:
          type: string
          description: Internal detail — only included for 5xx errors in non-production
        request_id: { type: string, format: uuid }
        timestamp:  { type: string, format: date-time }
        retry_after_seconds:
          type: integer
          description: Populated for RATE_LIMITED errors; client must wait before retrying
Authentication Standards
Two authentication mechanisms apply depending on the caller type:
Caller Type
Authentication Method
Token Validation
Human user via browser/mobile
OIDC Bearer token (Azure AD, Okta)
Validate JWT signature, audience, and expiry on every request — never trust claims without verification
Internal service (same network)
mTLS with workload identity certificate
Certificate issued by enterprise CA; validated by API gateway; SAN must match caller's registered service name
Partner / external system
OAuth 2.0 client credentials + API key rate limit layer
Client credentials scoped to minimum required API surface; API key used for rate-limiting tier, not authentication
CI/CD pipeline (e.g. eval jobs)
Short-lived OIDC token from CI provider (GitHub Actions / GitLab)
Trust CI OIDC provider; exchange for short-lived access token with limited scope and 1h TTL

API keys are never used as the sole authentication mechanism for production interfaces. They have no expiry, no revocation signal, and no user identity. API keys may layer on top of mTLS for rate limiting purposes but do not replace it.
Asynchronous Interface Standards

When an LLM response is routed to human review (latency > synchronous SLA), or when the GenAI system integrates with an event-driven pipeline, the async interface must follow the same standards as the synchronous one:
Event Schema: CloudEvents Envelope
All async events emitted by systems built from this blueprint use the CloudEvents 1.0 specification as the outer envelope:

{
  "specversion": "1.0",
  "type": "com.company.genai.query.completed",
  "source": "https://fnol-genai.company.internal/api/v1",
  "id": "evt-7b3f9a12-c4d2-4e8f-a1b0-2d5e7f8c9a0b",
  "time": "2026-07-01T05:10:00Z",
  "datacontenttype": "application/json",
  "correlationid": "corr-123e4567-e89b-12d3-a456-426614174000",
  "data": {
    "request_id": "req-abc12345",
    "session_id": "sess-xyz789",
    "query_hash": "sha256:3a7f...",
    "answer": "Based on policy section 4.2, the claim is covered for...",
    "citations": [
      { "doc_id": "pol-001", "title": "Motor Policy Schedule", "relevance_score": 0.94 }
    ],
    "confidence": 0.87,
    "routed_to_human_review": false,
    "latency_ms": 2340
  }
}
The CloudEvents envelope ensures that events from different GenAI systems are parseable by a generic event router, audit log, or monitoring pipeline without per-system handling code.
Idempotency and At-Least-Once Delivery

Event consumers must handle duplicate delivery. Every event includes the request ID as a stable idempotency key. Consumers track processed request IDs (in a Redis set or database unique index) and discard duplicates:

import redis
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class CloudEvent:
    id: str
    type: str
    source: str
    data: dict

class IdempotentEventProcessor:
    def __init__(self, redis_client: redis.Redis, ttl_seconds: int = 86400):
        self.redis = redis_client
        self.ttl = ttl_seconds

    def process(self, event: CloudEvent) -> bool:
        dedup_key = f"processed:{event.id}"
        # SET NX (set if not exists) is atomic — only one consumer processes each event
        was_new = self.redis.set(dedup_key, "1", ex=self.ttl, nx=True)
        if not was_new:
            return False  # duplicate — skip processing

        self._handle(event)
        return True

    def _handle(self, event: CloudEvent) -> None:
        match event.type:
            case "com.company.genai.query.completed":
                self._on_query_completed(event.data)
            case "com.company.genai.review.requested":
                self._on_review_requested(event.data)
            case _:
                pass  # unknown event type — log and ignore

    def _on_query_completed(self, data: dict) -> None:
        pass  # implementation: store result, trigger downstream

    def _on_review_requested(self, data: dict) -> None:
        pass  # implementation: enqueue in HITL review queue
API Versioning Strategy
GenAI interfaces evolve rapidly — new fields, new citation formats, new confidence signals. The blueprint prescribes a versioning strategy that allows evolution without breaking existing consumers:
Change Type
Version Impact
Handling
Add optional response field
Non-breaking
No version change; consumers ignore unknown fields (tolerant reader pattern)
Add required request field
Breaking
Minor version increment (/v2/); old version maintained for 6 months minimum
Remove or rename field
Breaking
Major version increment; old version receives sunset header; deprecated for 6 months then removed
Change field type or enum values
Breaking
Major version increment
Change authentication mechanism
Breaking
Coordinate migration window; dual support period minimum 3 months
Change event type name (CloudEvents)
Breaking
New event type alongside old; consumers migrated; old type sunset after 6 months
Consumer-Driven Contract Testing

Interface standards prevent unilateral breaking changes but do not catch accidental breakage introduced by a provider-side change that nobody noticed would affect consumers. Consumer-driven contract testing closes this gap: each consumer publishes a contract describing what it needs from the provider, and the provider verifies that contract in CI on every build.

# Consumer contract — using Pact (Python)
# This test lives in the consuming service's test suite

import pytest
from pact import Consumer, Provider

@pytest.fixture(scope="module")
def pact():
    pact_instance = Consumer("fnol-web-ui").has_pact_with(
        Provider("fnol-genai-api"),
        pact_dir="./pacts",
        publish_verification_results=True,
    )
    yield pact_instance
    pact_instance.stop()

def test_query_happy_path(pact):
    expected_response = {
        "answer": "The claim is covered under section 4.2 of the motor policy.",
        "citations": [
            {"doc_id": "pol-001", "title": "Motor Policy Schedule", "relevance_score": 0.94}
        ],
        "confidence": 0.87,
        "routed_to_human_review": False,
        "request_id": "req-abc12345",
        "latency_ms": 2340,
    }

    (pact
     .given("a valid claim query with high confidence")
     .upon_receiving("a query for motor policy coverage")
     .with_request(
         method="POST",
         path="/api/v2/query",
         headers={"Authorization": "Bearer <token>", "X-Correlation-ID": "test-corr-id"},
         body={"query": "Is the windscreen repair covered?", "session_id": "sess-1"},
     )
     .will_respond_with(
         status=200,
         headers={"Content-Type": "application/json"},
         body=expected_response,
     ))

    with pact:
        # Consumer makes the actual call to the Pact mock server
        import httpx
        response = httpx.post(
            pact.uri + "/api/v2/query",
            headers={"Authorization": "Bearer token", "X-Correlation-ID": "test-corr-id"},
            json={"query": "Is the windscreen repair covered?", "session_id": "sess-1"},
        )
        assert response.status_code == 200
        data = response.json()
        assert "answer" in data
        assert "citations" in data

The pact file generated by the consumer test is published to a Pact Broker. In the provider CI pipeline, the provider verifies all consumer contracts against its current implementation. A breaking change in the provider fails the provider CI build before it reaches production.
Blueprint Interface Registry

Each blueprint registers the interfaces it exposes in a machine-readable interface registry. This registry feeds the enterprise API catalogue, the contract testing broker, and the dependency graph that impact-assessments use when an upstream service changes:

# interfaces.yaml — part of the blueprint definition
# Published to: api-catalog.company.internal

interfaces:
  synchronous:
    - id: query-api
      protocol: REST/JSON
      version: v2
      path: /api/v2/query
      openapi_ref: schemas/openapi-v2.yaml
      auth: oidc-bearer
      sla:
        latency_p95_ms: 3000
        availability_pct: 99.5
      consumers:
        - fnol-web-ui
        - claims-mobile-app

    - id: streaming-query-api
      protocol: SSE
      version: v2
      path: /api/v2/query/stream
      openapi_ref: schemas/openapi-v2.yaml#/paths/~1v2~1query~1stream
      auth: oidc-bearer
      sla:
        time_to_first_token_p95_ms: 800
        availability_pct: 99.5

  asynchronous:
    - id: query-completed-event
      protocol: CloudEvents/Kafka
      topic: genai.query.completed
      asyncapi_ref: schemas/asyncapi-v1.yaml
      event_type: com.company.genai.query.completed
      producers:
        - fnol-genai-api
      consumers:
        - audit-log-pipeline
        - analytics-platform

    - id: review-requested-event
      protocol: CloudEvents/Kafka
      topic: genai.review.requested
      event_type: com.company.genai.review.requested
      consumers:
        - hitl-review-queue
Common Anti-Patterns in GenAI Interface Design
Anti-pattern
Risk
Correct Approach
Returning raw LLM output directly
Prompt injection via crafted input could cause the LLM to produce response JSON that overrides fields
Parse and reconstruct response from known-safe fields; never pass raw LLM output as JSON to client
Including full prompt in API response
Leaks system prompt, retrieved chunk content, and other context visible to the LLM
Return only answer and citation metadata; prompt and context are internal to the GenAI system
Using 200 OK for HITL pending state
Client cannot distinguish immediate answer from pending review
Use 202 Accepted with Location header pointing to status polling endpoint
No rate limiting on query endpoint
A single user or misconfigured client can exhaust LLM API budget
Rate limit per authenticated user (token bucket) and per client IP; return 429 with Retry-After header
Schema versioning in consumer code
Each consumer hard-codes version assumptions; provider cannot evolve
Provider publishes versioning headers; consumers use contract tests to detect incompatibilities in CI
Summary

Integration interface standards in blueprints prevent the most common source of integration failures in enterprise GenAI delivery: ad hoc, inconsistent interfaces that create security gaps, versioning debt, and coupling between systems that should be independent. The standard covers six layers — transport and protocol, authentication, request/response schema, async messaging, versioning, and contract testing — applied consistently to every system built from the blueprint.
Synchronous interfaces use REST/JSON with a standard request envelope, standard error codes, and OIDC or mTLS authentication.
Async events use the CloudEvents 1.0 envelope with idempotency keys and consumer-side deduplication.
Breaking changes trigger a version increment; old versions are maintained for a minimum deprecation period with Sunset headers.
Consumer-driven contract tests (Pact) run in provider CI — breaking changes fail CI before reaching production.
The blueprint interface registry makes every exposed interface discoverable by the enterprise API catalogue and impact assessment tooling.
